Us Epa Daily Max: 3.98 mg/L (40 CFR 433 metal finishing; total Ni, daily max)
Us Epa Monthly Avg: 2.38 mg/L (40 CFR 433 metal finishing; total Ni, monthly avg)
Eu Bat Ael: ≤0.5 mg/L (EU NFM BAT conclusions 2016/1032, Table 2, daily average; Ni)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Generally achievable with hydroxide precipitation + coag/floc + filtration at pH ~9–11; chelated Ni from some cleaners/complexants may require oxidation/breaking chelates, sulfide precipitation, or IX polishing (iminodiacetate/thiol resins). Potential driver if targeting EU BAT-AEL.

Us Epa Daily Max: 3.38 mg/L (40 CFR 433; total Cu, daily max)
Us Epa Monthly Avg: 2.07 mg/L (40 CFR 433; total Cu, monthly avg)
Eu Bat Ael: 0.05–0.5 mg/L (EU NFM BAT conclusions 2016/1032, Table 2, daily average; Cu)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Straightforward via hydroxide precipitation (or sulfide for very low residuals), then fine solids separation. Most challenging when aiming for sub-0.1 mg/L (requires excellent clarification/filtration; often needs IX or membrane polishing to avoid particulate carryover).

Us Epa Daily Max: 2.61 mg/L (40 CFR 433; total Zn, daily max)
Us Epa Monthly Avg: 1.48 mg/L (40 CFR 433; total Zn, monthly avg)
Eu Bat Ael: ≤1 mg/L (EU NFM BAT conclusions 2016/1032, Table 2, daily average; Zn)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Usually easy with hydroxide precipitation (pH ~9–10.5) and good solids separation. Can be limited by fine particulate Zn(OH)2 carryover; polishing filtration/UF helps.

Eu Bat Ael: 0.1–5 mg/L (EU STM BREF final draft May 2005; Fe)
Japan Wpca: 10 mg/L (Japan National Effluent Standards; dissolved iron)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Typically removed with the same hydroxide precipitation step used for other metals; not usually the limiting contaminant. Achieving <0.1 mg/L reliably generally requires high-rate clarification + filtration (or UF).

Japan Wpca: 10 mg B/L (non-coastal) or 230 mg B/L (coastal) (Japan National Effluent Standards)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Often a rate-limiting contaminant when low limits are imposed (e.g., 1 mg/L is cited as a stringent discharge limit). Not removed well by conventional metal hydroxide precipitation; typically needs boron-selective ion exchange (N-methylglucamine resins), multi-pass RO/NF, or advanced adsorption/co-precipitation schemes.

Eu Bat Ael: Up to 0.1 mg/L (some NFM production types NR); also shown as 0.1–0.5 mg/L for certain production types (EU NFM BAT conclusions 2016/1032, Table 2, daily average; Co)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Removed by hydroxide precipitation (often co-precipitates with Fe(OH)3); very low targets may require sulfide precipitation or chelating IX polishing. Watch for complexants from cleaners/coolants.

Us Epa Daily Max: 2.77 mg/L (40 CFR 433; total Cr, daily max)
Us Epa Monthly Avg: 1.71 mg/L (40 CFR 433; total Cr, monthly avg)
Eu Bat Ael: ≤0.2 mg/L (EU NFM BAT conclusions 2016/1032, Table 2, daily average; total Cr)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: If any hexavalent chromium present, must reduce Cr(VI)→Cr(III) (e.g., bisulfite/ferrous) before precipitation. Meeting sub-0.2 mg/L generally requires robust reduction control + filtration/polishing.

Eu Bat Ael: 10–20 mg/L (EU STM BREF final draft May 2005; F)
China Gb25467: 10 mg/L (China GB 8978-1996, Class 1; fluorides shown for a listed industry category within the GB 8978 table)
Japan Wpca: 8 mg F/L (non-coastal) or 15 mg F/L (coastal) (Japan National Effluent Standards)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Typically treated via calcium precipitation (CaF2) using lime/CaCl2; performance limited by solubility and by complexation (Al/F). Very low limits can require multi-stage precipitation + filtration, or RO/NF; scaling control critical.

Recommended Design Target: Not specifically regulated; propose internal target 0.10 mg/L (ΣREE)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Often not explicitly regulated as individual REEs; typically controlled indirectly via total suspended solids/total metals permit limits or site-specific permits. REEs precipitate readily as hydroxides/oxalates; the practical challenge is controlling colloids/fines and avoiding re-dissolution under low pH/complexants.

Eu Bat Ael: 5–30 mg/L (EU STM BREF final draft May 2005; suspended solids)
Japan Wpca: 200 mg/L (daily) and 150 mg/L (daily average) (Japan National Effluent Standards; SS)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: In metal-finishing style WWTPs, meeting low TSS is often the binding constraint because residual metals track with particulate carryover. Requires good floc formation, high-quality clarification (e.g., lamella), and polishing (sand filtration/UF).

Primarily an operational control requirement. Critical for downstream precipitation chemistry, corrosion control, and permit compliance; needs reliable online pH measurement + automated dosing with alkalinity/acid redundancy.

Eu Bat Ael: 100–500 mg/L (EU STM BREF final draft May 2005; COD)
Japan Wpca: 160 mg/L (daily) and 120 mg/L (daily average) (Japan National Effluent Standards; COD)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Driven by surfactants, coolant emulsions, and plating additives. Conventional metal precipitation does not remove soluble COD; may require emulsion breaking + DAF, carbon, or biological treatment (if biodegradable) depending on permit basis.

Japan Wpca: 160 mg/L (daily) and 120 mg/L (daily average) (Japan National Effluent Standards; BOD)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: BOD is typically from biodegradable organics (some surfactants/coolant components). If BOD/COD limits are tight, biological treatment or advanced oxidation may be needed; otherwise often not limiting in predominantly inorganic (metal-finishing) wastewaters.

Us Epa Daily Max: 52 mg/L (40 CFR 433; oil & grease, daily max)
Us Epa Monthly Avg: 26 mg/L (40 CFR 433; oil & grease, monthly avg)
Eu Bat Ael: Total hydrocarbons 1–5 mg/L (EU STM BREF final draft May 2005; total hydrocarbons as proxy for oil/grease)
Japan Wpca: 5 mg/L (Japan National Effluent Standards; N-hexane extracts, mineral oil)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: Often the first step constraint for machining wastewater; requires effective oil separation (API/coalescer) and typically DAF for emulsified oils. Emulsified coolants may require chemical cracking before DAF. Residual oil can foul downstream filters/IX/membranes.

Japan Wpca: 100 mg/L (as total of NH3-N×0.4 + NO2-N + NO3-N) (Japan National Effluent Standards)
Treatment Difficulty Notes: NH3-N usually not dominant in magnet manufacturing unless there is significant organic nitrogen (some additives) or biological treatment upset. If required, treat via nitrification (bio) or breakpoint chlorination/air stripping (less common industrially).
